perturbation.original_text: COMPANY will maintain accurate books and records with respect to the calculation of all payments due under this Agreement. The Licensors shall have the right, upon reasonable notice, to inspect COMPANY'S books and records and all other documents and material in COMPANY'S possession or control with respect to the Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which becomes the subject matter of this Agreement (and only with respect to Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which become the subject matter hereof).
perturbation.changed_text: COMPANY will maintain books and records with respect to the calculation of payments due under this Agreement. The Licensors may, with what COMPANY considers reasonable notice, inspect COMPANY'S books and records and other documents and material in COMPANY'S possession or control with respect to the Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which becomes the subject matter of this Agreement (and only with respect to Content each has or might license to CONTENT PROVIDER which become the subject matter hereof).
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear obligation for the Company to maintain accurate records and grants Licensors the right to inspect them with reasonable notice. The modified text introduces ambiguity by removing 'accurate' and adding 'with what COMPANY considers reasonable notice,' making the inspection right subjective and dependent on the Company's interpretation of 'reasonable'.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 1601 grants shareholders (and by extension, Licensors in this agreement who are receiving equity as consideration) the right to inspect a corporation's books and records for a proper purpose. By allowing the company to determine what constitutes 'reasonable notice,' the modified text potentially impedes the Licensor's ability to exercise their inspection rights effectively, thereby contradicting the intent and potentially the letter of the law. The law mandates providing access and information and this change imposes a subjective barrier.
perturbation.location: Section 6. RECORD INSPECTION, AUDIT AND INCONTESTABILITY PERIOD, Paragraph A

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 16. Rights of Inspection [1600 - 1605] ( Chapter 16 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 1601. (a) (1) The accounting books, records, and minutes of proceedings of the shareholders and the board and committees of the board of any domestic corporation, and of any foreign corporation keeping any records in this state or having its principal office in California, or a true and accurate copy thereof if the original has been lost, destroyed, or is not normally physically located within this state shall be open to inspection at the corporation’s principal office in California, or if none, at the physical location for the corporation’s registered agent for service of process in this state, upon the written demand on the corporation of any shareholder or holder of a voting trust certificate at any reasonable time during usual business hours, for a purpose reasonably related to the holder’s interests as a shareholder or as the holder of a voting trust certificate. (2) As an alternative to the procedure in subdivision (a), the shareholder or holder of a voting trust certif
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Corporations Code § 1601. The original text requires 'reasonable notice' for inspection of company records. The changed text allows COMPANY to determine what is 'reasonable notice,' potentially hindering the Licensors' ability to inspect records effectively. The scraped law snippet 1 states that accounting books and records 'shall be open to inspection... upon the written demand on the corporation of any shareholder... at any reasonable time during usual business hours, for a purpose reasonably related to the holder’s interests as a shareholder'. By allowing the company to subjectively define 'reasonable notice,' the licensor's right to inspect is weakened because the company can claim the licensor's 'reasonable time' is not actually reasonable. This imposes a barrier, undermining the law.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered by courier or other means of personal service (including by means of a nationally recognized courier service or professional messenger service), or sent by telex or telecopy or mailed first class, postage prepaid, by certified mail, return receipt requested, in all cases, addressed as indicated in the introductory recital of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests and other communications hereunder should preferably be in writing and delivered by means of communication that the sender deems appropriate, addressed as indicated in the introductory recital of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies precise methods for delivering notices, ensuring proof of delivery. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall' with 'should preferably' and substituting specific delivery methods with 'means of communication that the sender deems appropriate'. This creates uncertainty about whether written notice is mandatory and whether the recipient will reliably receive the notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 details how service by mail is effected, including requirements for proof of service. By making written notice and reliable delivery methods non-mandatory, the modified text could lead to disputes over whether notice was properly given, potentially conflicting with the procedural requirements and evidentiary standards for legal notices under California law, especially regarding deadlines and legal consequences tied to those deadlines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of Californi
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's mandate for delivering notices. The original text uses 'shall' and specifies delivery methods like courier, certified mail, etc., ensuring proof of delivery. The changed text uses 'should preferably' and 'means of communication that the sender deems appropriate,' making written notice non-mandatory and delivery methods uncertain. This contradicts the procedural requirements for legal notices, specifically regarding deadlines and evidence of service under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1013(a), states that 'the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office...in a sealed envelope, with postage paid'. The modified text's allowance for unspecified delivery methods and potentially non-written notice undermines the legal requirement for proper service, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereby agrees that any such court shall have in personam jurisdiction over it, consents to service of process in any manner prescribed or authorized by California Law, and agrees that a final judgment in any such action or proceeding shall be conclusive and may be enforced in other jurisdictions by suit on the judgment or in any other manner specified by Law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that any such court might have in personam jurisdiction over it, generally consents to service of process, and believes that a final judgment in any such action or proceeding should be persuasive and may be considered for enforcement in other jurisdictions.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a clear agreement on personal jurisdiction, consent to service of process, and the enforceability of judgments. The modified text weakens this by replacing 'agrees' with 'acknowledges...might have,' 'consents' with 'generally consents', and 'shall be conclusive and may be enforced' with 'should be persuasive and may be considered for enforcement'. This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty about the jurisdiction and enforceability of judgments.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Full Faith and Credit Clause, Article IV, Section 1, United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The Full Faith and Credit Clause of the U.S. Constitution requires states to respect the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state. By suggesting that a judgment 'should be persuasive' rather than 'shall be conclusive' and 'may be considered for enforcement' rather than 'may be enforced,' the modified text undermines the mandatory nature of the Full Faith and Credit Clause, implying that California judgments are not automatically entitled to recognition and enforcement in other jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article IV | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article IV Primary tabs Section 1. Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state. And the Congress may by general laws prescribe the manner in which such acts, records, and proceedings shall be proved, and the effect thereof. Section 2. The citizens of each state shall be entitled to all privileges and immunities of citizens in the several states. A person charged in any state with treason, felony, or other crime, who shall flee from justice, and be found in another state, shall on demand of the executive authority of the state from which he fled, be delivered up, to be removed to the state having jurisdiction of the crime. No person held to service or labor in one state, under the laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in consequence of any law or regulation therein, be discharged from such service or labor, but shall be delivered up on claim of the party to whom such service or labor may be due. Section 3. New states may be admitted by the Congress into this union; but no new states shall be formed or erected within the jurisdiction of any other state; nor any state be formed by the junction of two or more states, or parts of states, without the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned as well as of the Congress. The Congress shall have power to dispose of and make all needful rules and regulations respecting the territory or other property belonging to the United States; and nothing in this Constitution shall be so construed as to prejudice any claims of the United States, or of any particular state. Section 4. The United States shall guarantee to every state in this union a republican form of government, and shall protect each of them against invasion; and on application of the legislature, or of the executive (when the legislature cannot be convened) against domestic violence. ‹ Article III up Article V › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Ser
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the enforceability of judgments from California, suggesting they 'should be persuasive' rather than 'shall be conclusive,' and 'may be considered for enforcement' instead of 'may be enforced.' This undermines the Full Faith and Credit Clause, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, mandates that 'Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state.' By implying that California judgments are not automatically entitled to recognition and enforcement, the modified text contradicts this constitutional requirement.
